---
title: "Week 4 Lab: Exploring the National Health & Nutrition Examination Survey (NHANES)"
subtitle: "PSY 150: Lab Exercise "
author: "KEY"
date: "`r Sys.Date()`"
output: 
    html_document:
      theme: sketchy
---

```{r setup, echo=FALSE, warning=FALSE, message=FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(warning = FALSE, message = FALSE, eval=FALSE)
```

------------------------------------------------------------------------

### Outline

* Read in data
* Subset the data to make more manageable 
* Create outcome variable to analyze
* Split the data into 3 age groups 
* Examine variable distributions using boxplots 
* Create correlation tables for each age group
* Compare correlations & significance across groups 

------------------------------------------------------------------------

### Load packages

```{r, warning = FALSE, message = FALSE}

# Don't forget to load libraries! ( ▶️ )

library(psych)
library(tidyverse)
library(janitor)
library(here)
library(sjPlot)
library(GGally)
library(corrplot)
library(reshape2)

```


------------------------------------------------------------------------

Data source: *National Health and Nutrition Examination Survey (NHANES)* 

Link: https://www.cdc.gov/nchs/nhanes/index.html

------------------------------------------------------------------------

### Read in the `NHANES` data

```{r}

nhanes_all <- read_csv(here("data", "all_vars_nhanes_2017_2018.csv"))

```

### Create a subset of the data named `nhanes_clean`

```{r}
nhanes_clean <- nhanes_all %>%
  select(1:9,            # Demographics
         contains(c(                     
         "phq9",         # Depression scale items
         "emotion",      
         "activity",
         "recreation",
         "walk",
         "sleep",
         "pain",
         "outdoors"))) 

```

### Create another data.frame named `activity_data`

```{r}
activity_data <- nhanes_clean %>% 
  select(1:9,                     
         contains(c("phq9", "outdoors_","days", "sleep_hours"))) %>% 
  mutate(across(contains("phq9"),
    ~ case_when(
      .x == "Not at all" ~ 0,
      .x == "Several days" ~ 1,
      .x == "More than half the days" ~ 2,
      .x == "Nearly every day" ~ 3,
      TRUE ~ NA_real_))) %>% 
   mutate(depression_scale = rowMeans(across(contains("phq9")), na.rm = TRUE)) %>% 
   mutate(across(outdoors_workday:mod_recreation_days,
    ~ case_when(
      .x %in% c(99, 3333, 7777, 9999) ~ NA_real_ ,
      TRUE ~ as.numeric(.x)
    ))) %>% 
   drop_na(depression_scale)
  
```


------------------------------------------------------------------------

### Prepare final analysis data

------------------------------------------------------------------------

Split the sample by age range by making 3 data.frames
```{r}

make_corr_data <- function(min_age, max_age) {
  activity_data %>% 
    filter(age_years >= min_age & age_years <= max_age) %>% 
    select(
      depression_scale, sex_female,
      contains(c("outdoors_", "walk_or_bike_days", "days", "sleep_hours"))) %>%
    mutate(across(where(~ is.character(.x) | is.factor(.x)), ~ as.numeric(as.factor(.x)))) 
}

# create datasets
cor_18_20 <- make_corr_data(18, 20)
cor_18_24 <- make_corr_data(18, 24)
cor_30_60 <- make_corr_data(30, 60)

```


------------------------------------------------------------------------

### Quartiles & Boxplots

------------------------------------------------------------------------

![](figures/boxplot_diagram.jpg)

### What is a quartile?

- Takes a variables distribution and cuts it into 4 equal parts
- 1st quartile (Q1) = The value below which 25% of the data fall
- 2nd quartile (Q2) = The value below which 50% of the data fall (the median)
- 3rd quartile (Q3) = The value below which 75% of the data fall.
- Interquartile Range (IQR = Q3 – Q1) = The middle 2 quartiles (50% of dist.)

### What is a boxplot?

- Dark line (inside the box) = median (50th percentile)
- Box-length (IQR) = middle 50% of data (Q3 -Q1)
- Left side of the box (Q1) = 25th percentile 
- Right side of the box (Q3) = 75th percentile
- Whiskers = 1.5 × IQR
- Points beyond whiskers = Outliers

------------------------------------------------------------------------

### Take a quick look at the variables 

Check multiple distributions at once 

------------------------------------------------------------------------

```{r}

cor_18_20 |> 
  select(-sex_female, -sleep_hours_weekday, -mod_recreation_days) |> 
  melt() |> 
  ggplot(aes(x=value, label=variable, fill=variable)) +   
  #geom_histogram() + # UNCOMMNET to plot histograms (& COMMENT line below)
  geom_boxplot() + 
  facet_wrap(~variable, scales = "free") +
  guides(fill = "none")

```

------------------------------------------------------------------------

### Correlation Strength (rules of thumb)

- Small (weak): r ≈ .10
- Medium (moderate): r ≈ .30
- Large (strong): r ≈ .50

**Reference:** Cohen, J. (1988). *Statistical power analysis for the behavioral sciences.*

------------------------------------------------------------------------

### Create correlation tables

------------------------------------------------------------------------

Correlations for survey respondents ages 18 - 20
```{r}

cor_18_20 %>% 
tab_corr(
  corr.method = "pearson",
  na.deletion = "pairwise",
  triangle    = "lower",
  digits      = 2,
  show.p      = TRUE,
  p.numeric   = TRUE,  # print (p-value) / (*) notation use 'FALSE' 
  title = "Age range: 18 - 20 (N = 310)"
)

```

**Question 1:** Interpret the correlation between `depression_scale` & `sex_female` (direction, strength, significance)

**Response:** __________________________________________


**Question 2:** Why is the correlation for `depression_scale` & `outdoors_nonwork` marked as not significant?

**Response:** __________________________________________

Correlations for survey respondents ages 18 - 24
```{r}

cor_18_24 %>% 
tab_corr(
  corr.method = "pearson",
  na.deletion = "pairwise",
  triangle    = "lower",
  digits      = 2,
  show.p      = TRUE,
  p.numeric   = TRUE,
  title = "Age range: 18 - 24 (N = 597)"
)

```

**Question 3:** What are two reasons which can explain why the correlations are now significant in this new sample (column 1)?

**Response:** __________________________________________

Correlations for survey respondents ages 30 - 60
```{r}

cor_30_60 %>% 
tab_corr(
  corr.method = "pearson",
  na.deletion = "pairwise",
  triangle    = "lower",
  digits      = 2,
  show.p      = TRUE,
  p.numeric   = TRUE,
  title = "Age range: 30 - 60 (N = 2423)" 
)

```

**Question 4:** Compare significant correlations (column 1) with the previous table: What changed across the two samples?

**Response:** __________________________________________


------------------------------------------------------------------------

### Visualize correltation strength and direction using `corrplot()`

------------------------------------------------------------------------

```{r}
corr_matrix <- cor(cor_30_60, use = "pairwise.complete.obs", method = "pearson")

corrplot(
  corr_matrix, 
  method = "color",   
  type = "upper", diag = FALSE,       
  tl.col = "black", tl.srt = 35)
```


------------------------------------------------------------------------

### A quick way to visualize multiple variables using `ggpairs()`

------------------------------------------------------------------------

```{r}

activity_data %>% 
  filter(age_years >= 30 & age_years <= 60) %>% 
  select(depression_scale, sex_female, outdoors_workday,
         outdoors_nonwork, sleep_hours_weekend) %>% 
  ggpairs(
    aes(alpha = 0.1),
    lower = list(continuous = "smooth"))

```

**Question:** Do you think the NHANES survey data has any selection bias?

**Response:** __________________________________________
